title: Re-evaluate /api/tracks SQL pattern at 10k tracks
description: Per CLAUDE.md, the current 'fetch all rows then filter in Python' beats SQLAlchemy IN-filter on SQLCipher at ~3k tracks. Verify whether this still holds at 10k; if not, switch to indexed lookups and document the breakpoint.
acceptanceCriteria: Benchmark in tests/perf/test_tracks_sql.py measures: (a) fetch-all-then-filter approach (current); (b) SQL IN(content_ids) approach; on a 10k sandbox library.
Results are written to .agent/prd/PERFORMANCE_NOTES.md with timings.
If IN-filter wins at 10k: refactor /api/tracks to use it; preserve a comment 'breakpoint determined empirically at TASK-024' and the timing data.
If fetch-all-then-filter still wins: leave code alone, update CLAUDE.md note to extend the claim to 10k.
Decision is reviewable: the benchmark script is checked in and re-runnable.

steps.details: tests/e2e/sandbox-10k.db (or generated via a fixture from the existing sandbox). 10k DjmdContent rows, 50k DjmdSongHistory rows, 30k DjmdSongMyTag rows. If generating, document the seed in the fixture.
tests/perf/test_tracks_sql.py: implement fetch_all_then_filter() and in_filter() variants; time each over 20 iterations; report min/median/p95.
Append to .agent/prd/PERFORMANCE_NOTES.md a section 'TASK-024 results' with absolute timings and the chosen path. Include the date and pyrekordbox version.
If in_filter wins by > 1.5x at 10k, switch /api/tracks. Update the CLAUDE.md note to reflect the new approach. If fetch-all wins, leave code untouched; update CLAUDE.md to extend the claim to 10k.

technicalNotes: Per CLAUDE.md: 'For full-library page loads (~3k rows) the IN filter is slower than fetch-all-then-filter against pyrekordbox's SQLCipher. Do not optimize this back to .filter(...ContentID.in_(row_ids)).' This task tests if that breakpoint shifts at 10k.
Run the benchmark with the master.db file's OS page cache warm and cold - report both. Warm is the realistic case.
